---
title: "LRPMM101: Prior sensitivity for the correlated-domain-factor measurement model"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

{{< include _partials/_header.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_gate_badge.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_key_findings.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_reading_guide.qmd >}}

## Overview

LRPMM101 is the **prior-sensitivity companion** to [LRPMM01](../lrp-rli-mm-001). It is the
same model, the same data and the same sampler settings, differing in exactly one
thing: the **loading and residual priors**.

| | LRPMM01 (reference) | LRPMM101 (this model) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| loading $\lambda_j$ | $\text{HalfNormal}(1)$ | $\text{TruncatedNormal}(0.6,\ 0.5,\ \text{lower}=0)$ |
| residual $\sigma_j$ | $\text{HalfNormal}(1)$ | $\text{HalfNormal}(0.5)$ |
| everything else | — | identical, including `target_accept` = 0.999 |

Holding the sampler equal is deliberate: the prior is the **single free variable**, so
any difference in the posterior is attributable to it and to nothing else.

## Why this model exists

The convergence fix for LRPMM01 (see
`notes/202607101638-mm-001-convergence-reparameterisation.md`) originally did two things
at once: it **marginalised** the factor scores out of the Gaussian measurement likelihood,
*and* it **recalibrated** these priors. It then reported the posterior as unchanged, "only
the sampler geometry" having moved.

Only the first half of that is true. The conjugate rewrite **is** measure-preserving. A
prior change is not — so the two were confounded, and neither could be assessed on its own.
LRPMM101 separates them.

## What the ablation found

A 2×2 over {original, recalibrated} priors × {0.95, 0.999} `target_accept`, reporting tier
(6 chains × 6000 draws):

| priors | `target_accept` = 0.95 | `target_accept` = 0.999 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| original $\text{HalfNormal}(1)$ | **FAIL** — 571 divergences, min ESS 370 | **PASS** — 0 divergences, min ESS 2200 |
| recalibrated | **FAIL** — 528 divergences, min ESS 850 | **PASS** — 0 divergences, min ESS 1700 |

The prior recalibration is therefore **neither necessary nor sufficient** for convergence:
at 0.95 both prior sets fail by a comparable margin, and at 0.999 both pass. What actually
clears the gate is the **marginalisation** (which repairs the energy diagnostic, BFMI
0.21 → ≈ 0.87) plus the **raised `target_accept`** (which clears the boundary divergences
the strict gate requires to be zero).

And the two posteriors agree to within Monte-Carlo error — factor correlations within 0.02,
every structural slope to the third decimal. The data dominate the prior.

Meanwhile the recalibration is **not free**. It moves the prior-implied median communality
from **0.50 to 0.79**, and $P(\text{communality} > 0.8)$ from **0.29 to 0.49**. With only two
indicators per factor at $n \approx 51$ that is a substantive prior commitment about how well
the indicators measure their domain — which is precisely the quantity the model exists to
estimate. Buying nothing and costing that, the recalibration was **reverted** in LRPMM01 and
is retained here as the sensitivity fit.

_Measurement / triangulation, NOT causal._ As with LRPMM01: per the locked DAG's **ID-2**, every factor → gain slope is a
**latent-ability-confounded adjusted association**. The randomised causal claim lives in the
ITT suite (`lrp-rli-itt-010` for word reading) and the `gain_factors` randomised term, not
here.

![Model diagram](model_graph.png){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

{{< include _partials/_priors.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_prior_predictive.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_results_corr_factor.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_technical.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_footer.qmd >}}
